claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'm here to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions.

Let's start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children. I'm inquiring about both of them - Alex, who is 6 years old, and Blake, who is 3 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have two children - Alex (6 years old) and Blake (3 years old).

Now, let me ask: **Do you currently live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to understand your living situation with the children.

**Do Alex and Blake currently live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they both live with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! That's very helpful. 

Since both Alex and Blake are under 16 and live with you in the UK, you appear to meet the basic criteria for Child Benefit.

Just to confirm a few final details: **Are you or your partner (if you have one) already receiving Child Benefit for Alex and Blake, or is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for either of them?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them, and I'm not currently receiving it either. That's why I'm inquiring about it now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent, thank you for confirming that.

One last question to complete the assessment: **Are either Alex or Blake currently in hospital, residential care, or living away from home for any extended period?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Blake has actually been in hospital for a few months now. It's been quite difficult.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to hear that Blake has been in hospital - that must be challenging for your family.

To properly assess your eligibility for Blake, I need to understand the situation better: **How long has Blake been in hospital, and are you regularly spending money on Blake (for example, on clothes, toys, or other items for them)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake has been in hospital for about 13 weeks now. Unfortunately, I haven't been able to afford to spend money on their needs while they're in there - it's been financially very tight for us.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that information, and I understand how difficult this situation must be for you and your family.

Based on the information you've provided, let me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Assessment Complete

Based on the information you've provided, here's your Child Benefit eligibility:

**For Alex (age 6): ✓ ELIGIBLE**
You qualify for Child Benefit for Alex. Alex is under 16, lives with you in the UK, you're responsible for them, and there are no circumstances affecting their eligibility.

**For Blake (age 3): ✗ NOT ELIGIBLE (currently)**
Unfortunately, you do not qualify for Child Benefit for Blake at this time. Blake has been in hospital for 13 weeks, which exceeds the 12-week limit. After 12 weeks in hospital, Child Benefit usually stops unless you're regularly spending money on the child. Since you've mentioned you haven't been able to afford to spend money on Blake's needs during their hospital stay, the exception doesn't apply.

**What this means:** You can claim Child Benefit for Alex, but not for Blake while they remain in hospital beyond the 12-week period without you making regular financial contributions toward their needs.

If Blake's circumstances change (for example, if they come home from hospital, or if you're able to start regularly spending money on them), you may be able to claim Child Benefit for Blake at that point. You can contact HMRC to discuss your situation if things change.

I hope this helps clarify your situation, and I wish Blake a full recovery.

reasonings: Alex is 6 years old, lives with you in the UK, and is under 16. You are responsible for Alex as they live with you. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and there are no circumstances affecting their eligibility (such as being in hospital or care). Therefore, you qualify for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 3 years old and normally lives with you in the UK. However, Blake has been in hospital for 13 weeks, which exceeds the 12-week limit set by the rules. After 12 weeks in hospital, you are no longer entitled to Child Benefit unless you regularly spend money on the child. You have confirmed that you have not been able to afford to spend money on Blake's needs while they are in hospital. Therefore, you do not qualify for Child Benefit for Blake at this time.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with two children: Alex (6) and Blake (3). They meet the basic eligibility criteria as they live in the UK and are responsible for both children. However, Blake has been in hospital for 13 weeks, which exceeds the 12-week limit after which Child Benefit normally stops. The claimant has not been regularly spending money on Blake during the hospital stay, so the exception to continue receiving Child Benefit does not apply. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex but not for Blake.

Blake is 3 years old and normally lives with you in the UK. However, Blake has been in hospital for 13 weeks, which exceeds the 12-week limit set by the rules. You have confirmed that you have not been able to afford to spend money on Blake's needs while they are in hospital. Therefore, you do not qualify for Child Benefit for Blake at this time.

The claimant has not been regularly spending money on Blake during the hospital stay, so the exception to continue receiving Child Benefit does not apply. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex but not for Blake.
